Transcrição Completa: 48 Becoming EXTREMELY productive and efficient 2 8 25
[00:00] "Okay, so we had a question from uh from Daniel in the inner circle group chat for our call this week. Uh he says, 'You have an insane ability to execute, bring to life new ideas while growing your own businesses and clients businesses. Would love to know how you plan your days, weeks, months. Do you time block daily, weekly, monthly? How do you incorporate new ideas, projects into existing endeavors? What's your structure of time allocation towards deep work, client work, systems, new ideas, personal time, etc.'"
[00:45] "Um, not sure if this is worthy of a group call. So, yeah, definitely worthy of a group call. There's a lot of cool stuff that we figured out over the years and it all really boils down to efficiency."
[01:00] "So, allow me to begin with some simple things that u I encourage you all to start off with. Okay. So when you're essentially looking to accomplish two main things, okay, the the underlying question that Daniel essentially is proposing is is productivity and efficiency."
[01:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Productivity Definition"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Definição de produtividade sendo mostrada, foco em "rate of output per unit of input"
[01:25] "Okay, so productivity, let's just go and collectively we'll look it up here online together so we can check it out. Um, and let's get to definitions. This is one thing that I learned back in the day when I worked with Mr. Grant Cardone was always look at the definitions of things."
[01:42] "So the state or quality of producing something, the effectiveness of productive effort, especially in industry as measured in terms of the rate of output per unit of input. So this is the key definition here. Keep that in mind."
[02:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Efficiency Definition"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Definição de eficiência destacando "maximum productivity with minimum wasted effort or expense"
[02:05] "And then let's also go look up efficiency. So this the an action designed to achieve efficiency. The state of being efficient. Let's look up efficient. Therefore, bang. Okay. Uh achieving maximum productivity. Okay. With minimum wasted effort or expense."
[02:30] "So when you first of all think of just these two terms, essentially what it all boils down to for what you have to think with on these things and it's very simple. In order to achieve these two things as outcomes, we have to think as essentially how can we put very little in and get the most out."
[02:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Core Principle: Input vs Output"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito visual de minimizar input e maximizar output, representação de eficiência
[02:55] "Okay, that's all it comes down to. If we can think with this and actually achieve this, we are therefore productive and efficient. We we maximize productivity and efficiency the more that we can do this again and again and again."
[03:15] "So essentially what most people who aren't as efficient as they want to be end up struggling with is they're putting too much in and they're either getting too little out or they're putting too much in and they're getting the outcome that they want but they're unhappy with what they're putting in."
[03:35] "Okay? So I want to be very clear when I say this just to start. The whole concept that you're trying to achieve when you become more productive and more efficient all boils down to focusing on the input more than the output."
[03:55] "Okay, a lot of people get caught up thinking that you pay attention to the output. The output is a goal and an outcome. Okay, the input is what we can control and what we can influence and what we can constrain."

[04:40] "So, we have to have something that we can look at that just makes us question our capacity to perform. Okay? It makes us look at the amount of input that we are putting into things and the output we get in return and just say like fuck dude I'm clearly doing something wrong."

[07:20] "Uh none of the materials existed to be able to build that rocket. Okay. Like the engineering that went into building it required them to create new metals, required them to like do things that again just had never been accomplished in this day and age in in the history of man."

VISUAL: Elon desafiando a equipe, representação de confronto entre justificativas e possibilidades

I'm gonna give you guys six months to accomplish it.' And obviously all the mathematicians, physicists, rocket scientists, and engineers threw a fucking nerd fit, okay?"
[08:55] "And they were like, Elon, it's just math. And and and this is what happens to people all the time. Like they argue against a time constraint, okay? So Elon's telling them like, 'Look, I don't give a fuck if you've mathematically quantified that it's going to take a decade. I don't care what your justifications are.'"
[09:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Justifications Are The Devil"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[09:45] "And again, the team just refuts says no, it's impossible. And they go through this whole fucking storyline where they're using justifications. This this is a very key point that I want to make like very known. Okay, justifications, let me make sure I spell that right. Justifications are the devil."
[10:10] "Okay, they are the reason that most people remain unproductive. They are the reason that most people never achieve true efficiency and maximize output. Okay? Because they go through life justifying why things are the way they are for them."
[10:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Parkinson's Law"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de time constraints, representação de como restrições de tempo forçam eficiência
[10:35] "and they justify like the reasons that they want whatever it is to take the rate the time frame it's going to take or the required input it supposedly is going to take to get the outcome that they think and if again if you just look at it like all Elon did was just say get fucked I'm going to fire you"
[11:00] "So, what Elon chose to do is give them a time constraint. Okay? And by giving them a time constraint, what it does is it forces the brain to rethink how they're going to go about accomplishing it. Okay? So, it all starts off, this is called Parkinson's law to be clear."
[11:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ads Manager Example"
NÚMEROS: 8 horas/dia vs 4-6 horas/mês
VISUAL: Comparação extrema de eficiência, mostrando redução drástica de tempo

[11:50] "'Oh, Jeremy, like I'm sitting in the ads manager like eight hours a day, you know, like I hear you fucking gloat about you sitting in an ads manager for like four to six hours a month for a client. How the fuck are you doing that?' And and still making a fuckload more money than I make on any of my client deals."
[12:15] "And I tell him, I'm like, it's just a time constraint. Like all the things that you think you need to do in eight hours a day, which is really just nothing. Like you're doing literally nothing work. I've only given myself, you know, a static amount of time per week."

[25:10] "As a result, you don't just randomly accomplish shit faster. You have to force a time constraint onto what you do in order to unlock the new ideas that are necessary to execute with to get it done faster as a result."
[25:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Client Webinar Example"

VISUAL: Processo de negociação de timeline, resistência vs aceitação
[25:40] "Had a client, it was a consulting client of ours, uh, Airbnb arbitrage offer, and they were coming out with a second product, just like a more like general investment type of offer. And, uh, we wanted to create a new webinar for it."
[26:05] "The webinar model for them is like extremely successful. You know, we can spend like maybe 100K a month on it and it it kicks out like 500 to 700K every month. Now, it's really good. Now, for this second offer, obviously the best bet was let's do a webinar for it."
[26:30] "So, you know, we get to this point where we start talking about how long it's going to take. And right away, just like the default answer was, oh, it'll probably take about a month. And I'm like, what the fuck are you talking about? It's going to take a month."

It's like that literally to me sounds like maybe one day worth of actions max."
[27:25] "And then it's like, well, obviously you got to factor in your existing schedule. You got to factor in like the guy, okay, well, all of a sudden the guy's going to travel in the first part of the month, you know, and like, oh, the videographer is like over here or over there."
[27:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Timeline Negotiation"
NÚMEROS: 1/30 da fração inicial
VISUAL: Processo de redução progressiva de tempo: mês → semana → 3 dias
[27:50] "And it's like, oh, well, there's these other initiatives that are going on, you know, and it's like, okay, so instead of a day, uh, could we stretch it out to like two hours a day for a week, you know, like it can't fucking take a month."
[28:10] "And all of a sudden that answer was like widely accepted. It was like okay so it can't take a day but it could take a week. So it's like here's my point. What initially was determined to take a month I challenged to be 1/30th of that."
[28:35] "So, it's like again that was 1/30th in terms of the fraction there of the time that they initially said it was going to take. So, then they all agreed, okay, well, could we give it a week instead? And all of a sudden, everybody was like, yeah, that's cool."

[31:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Funnel Building Reality"
NÚMEROS: 30 minutos (opt-in page), 30 minutos (confirmation page)
VISUAL: Quebra de tarefas em componentes mensuráveis e rápidos
[31:05] "And I'm like, 'Oh, that's ridiculous. I already have two opt-in pages for webinars that are at plus 40% opt-in rates. You're just going to model those. How hard would it be to just model those? How long would that take if I gave you two pages you could copy to the tea?'"
[31:30] "And then all of a sudden, the answer went from like, 'Oh, I don't know if I could do it that fast to, uh, I could probably knock that out in like 30 minutes.' You see, and it's like, okay, well, how long would the confirmation page take?"
[31:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Belief In Time Constraint"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Ênfase na crença coletiva, todos devem acreditar na possibilidade
[31:55] "Again, I have two confirmation pages already done. What do you need for the confirmation pages that we don't already have that might need created? Oh, we already have everything that could be used. Great. We already got our answer."
[32:15] "Now, here's the point and this is this is very important to understand as like a followup to what I'm saying with the time constraints. Okay? You have to believe in the time constraint being possible. Okay? If you do not believe in the time constraint or your organization or team does not believe in the time constraint, it will not be accomplished."
[32:40] "Everybody has to believe. Okay? Everybody has to believe. And when I talk about tech, by the way, so this team in particular, they were using go high level to build their funnels and the and the specific funnel that was built that they were going to copy from was in HubSpot."
[33:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Software Solution Found"
NÚMEROS: $50 (custo)
VISUAL: Google search revelando ferramenta que copia funnels automaticamente
[33:10] "And they were like, 'Oh, well, you know, it might take me a little longer because like I have to build each element out.' And I was like, 'No, no, let's Google real quick if there's a tool that can copy a HubSpot funnel into a GoHighle funnel with a click of a button.'"
[33:30] "And lo and behold, there was like a fucking $50 software that already existed where literally in one click of a button, you could just copy any funnel that existed and it would just imported into go high level with all the elements predefined and everything and it did it almost perfectly."
[33:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Working Harder vs Working Smart"
NÚMEROS: 10 anos vs 8 meses
VISUAL: Conceito de que pessoas comuns equacionam velocidade com trabalho mais difícil
[34:00] "So again, leverage tech talent density, right? The right people sitting there doing it, time constraints, some fucking leader, and this is the important part to understand. I don't know what the tool was. Daniel, look it up yourself. Long story short, we found it in a Google search."
[34:25] "All right. Now, long here's my point, okay? We have to understand a very simple thing. When we go to accomplish something, it's going to be the regular people working with us that are going to be the ones that have the most push back to it being possible in a tight time frame."
[34:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B&C Players vs A Players"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Diferença de mentalidade: B&C odeiam velocidade, A players abraçam desafios

[35:20] "Think about it like this, right? In the Elon story that I talked about, they were going to work for 10 years. 10 years they were going to work. Instead, they had to work for eight months. And guess what? They didn't work harder in 8 months."
[35:45] "That's the thing you have to understand. It's like they're not technically working harder. They're leveraging tech. Like, if you look at that Department of Government efficiency with big balls and the guy who decoded the scroll, it's like, dude, these guys aren't working harder."

[1:02:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Probability Weighting"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Pesar probabilidade de resultado vs tempo necessário para testes
[1:02:55] "So it's like although yes, I could do all those split tests, think about it in terms of weight. Okay, if I just switch to a webinar like I chose to do, I technically split tested a conversion mechanism as my split test."
[1:03:20] "Why? Because the density of all those little tiny tests was far less dense, far less probable comparatively to just I'm just going to try a webinar instead. Let's just test out a webinar. That's likely a faster result because it's going to be one big test."

[1:05:35] "I'm not just weighing the probability of result. I'm also weighing in the time equation because remember what I just harped on there about five minutes ago. What did I say? [1:06:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Strategic Sequencing"
NÚMEROS: Centenas de milhares em lucro (webinar), depois voltar para call funnel
VISUAL: Ordem estratégica: lucro primeiro, depois testes com "house money"
[1:06:35] "You know, we turn the money into more money and we got a good rev share off bit in the first month, which is always exciting. Okay, but my point is is like all the little split tests, the reason, yes, are the split tests probable to work on the call funnel? Yeah, absolutely they are."
[1:07:00] "Would I have to split test many different things to eventually find what's going to work? Yeah. Is that easier to justify when the when the call funnel is a side piece funnel in that example instead of the main piece? Yeah."
[1:07:25] "Meaning, simply put, hear me out. Now that we're at a couple hundred grand in straight profit for the month from the webinar and we have a proven model that we'll repeat next month, it's like, doesn't it make sense to just take some of that profit and burn it on getting the call funnel to work now?"
[1:07:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "House Money Testing"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Testar com lucro gerado, não com investimento inicial do cliente
[1:07:55] "Doesn't it make more sense to now go back to the call funnel and start the split testing process? Think about the order there. So, it's like it's not like we neglect it and just kill it forever. It's like, well, no, let's pivot to something that has a very high probability to get the result we're after first"
[1:08:20] "and then when we have some profit, we have a nice profit buffer, then we go back to burning because we're just playing with house money over here on those split tests now. And the client won't give nearly as much of a fuck about burning that money because again, it's house money at that point."
[1:08:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Variable Thinking Framework"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Framework completo: input mínimo, output máximo, todas as variáveis consideradas

[1:09:15] "This is the literal first thing that we must think. How can I put literally as little as I possibly can in get the most out? Just like in that webinar example I gave. That's all I was really thinking with."

[1:38:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "No Justifications Answer"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[1:39:05] "That's the concept. That's the whole point is justification. I said I said in writing justifications are the devil. Okay? I don't use justifications. I don't I don't try to justify why it should take a week instead of a day."
[1:39:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Day and a Half vs Week"
NÚMEROS: 1 dia (tentativa) → 1.5 dias (resultado) vs 1 semana (justificada)
VISUAL: Preferir tentar em 1 dia e levar 1.5 que justificar 1 semana
[1:39:35] "I will try I'd rather try to accomplish it in a day and have it take a day and a half than justify that it should take a week. That's it. I'm just trying to make an extreme constraint and then acting as though that's what it could take"
[1:40:00] "and then saying, 'Okay, well, if it could take that, what would I do if it if it could take that?' Like, what would I think with what would the plan be if it had to be that time frame? And then your brain will magically unlock new ways to think about accomplishing it as a result of the time constraint."
[1:40:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Staff Justifications Example"

You don't want something to come up and be like, oh, like let me give you a perfect example. [1:40:55] "and like so therefore this can't be done until that time. You know, that's a lot of like the quote unquote justifications that happen on things. But it's like again, in reality, it's it's uh you just do stuff. You know what I mean? Just fucking do stuff and try to accomplish it extremely fast."

[1:42:20] "You do want to be aware of the parameters of the game you're playing. Like certain parameters of the game will be made you'll be made aware of them as you go and try to take action. [1:42:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Philippines VA Internet Example"
NÚMEROS: 50 videos de 10GB cada
VISUAL: Exemplo de limitação real: internet ruim do VA nas Filipinas
[1:42:50] "But it's like in almost all instances, those are pretty much just like literally the laws of the earth, you know? There's very little like if you if I if I had a VA in the Philippines that had like a fucking shitty ass internet connection, it's like okay, one of the time constraints like the the the laws of the game in their case would be like"
[1:43:15] "well, if I told that person to download like 50 10 gigabyte videos, it's probably going to take them mathematically like literally weeks to be able to just download the fucking videos, let alone re-upload them again when I need them to upload them back to us, you know?"
[1:43:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Swap The Player Solution"
NÚMEROS: 10 gigabytes up/down (fiber optic)
VISUAL: Solução: trocar pessoa, não justificar limitação
[1:43:45] "So, that just doesn't make sense in that example. I have to find a different player in the game because like if I use my internet, I have a dedicated fiber optic line. I have 10 gigabytes up and down. It's like I can accomplish the same exact thing in like a literal fraction of the time because that's a rule of the game in that exam."
[1:44:10] "You see what I mean? That's a person issue. It's a person issue and an technically an internet issue. We don't necessarily have to blame the person in the Philippines. It's their internet, not them. But regardless, my point is is like again, that's that's an issue of the person. I can just swap the person, my problem solved."

[2:17:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "900K+ Celebration Response"
NÚMEROS: $900K+ (dois meses seguidos), objetivo de $1M
VISUAL: Sem sorriso, sem celebração por chegar perto mas não atingir meta
[2:17:40] "Yeah. It's like I remember when James hit like 900 something K like two months in a row and like his team was pretty happy about it. Like I didn't even smile. I was just like, I can't even believe you guys celebrate, you know?"
[2:18:05] "Like, you came that close to a goal and like, you still didn't push over the goal. Like, what a fucking shame, you know? Like, good job on getting that far, but like seriously, I mean, come on. You know, somebody's got to give a fuck"

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
10 anos → 6 meses → 8 meses (Elon's rocket timeline)
8 horas/dia vs 1 hora/semana (ads manager efficiency)
1 mês → 1 semana → 3 dias (webinar funnel timeline)
$1,200 gasto, 5 leads não qualificados (call funnel)
$70K budget → $400K+ resultado primeiro mês (webinar pivot)
$100K/mês gasto → $500-700K resultado (webinar model)
$542,000/mês (Pierre & Brez, 20 anos)
$5K/mês investimento no inner circle
$1M/mês objetivo até abril
4 pessoas abaixo de 25 anos (DOGE team)
1 pessoa com 19 anos (Big Balls)
$1M prêmio (scroll decoding challenge)
40%+ opt-in rate (webinar pages)

30 minutos (opt-in page build time)
30 minutos (confirmation page build time)

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Parkinson's Law (time constraints)
Financial modeling antes de execução
Leverage tech constantemente
Small teams, high talent density
Scenario-based interview questions
Express mission primeiro
Vet clients no sales process
Pivot strategy (call funnel → webinar)
Test density analysis
House money testing approach
Strategic sequencing (profit first, then tests)
Template/data leverage (funnels testados)
Charismatic leadership approach
Firm but respectful communication
No attachment to people/staff
Move wrong people out immediately
Belief requirement (everyone must believe)
Timer strategy (start timer, show it)
Google for solutions first
Copy proven funnels, não criar do zero
Use AI models (ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity)
Leverage video game physics engines
Use automotive technology for new applications
International talent hiring platforms
Resourcefulness (build ladder vs stand on pile)
ESTRUTURA:
Inner Circle Group ($5K/mês)
DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) - 4 pessoas
Consulting clients model
Webinar funnel structure
Call funnel structure
Rev share agreements
Launch timeline: 2-4 horas
Weekly client time: 1 hora máximo
Monthly ads manager time: 4-6 horas
Team meetings: Mondays
Follow-up system em dias/semanas
Vet process no sales
Mission-first organization structure
A players vs B&C players framework
Dedicated fiber optic line (infrastructure)
VA teams internationally
Staff longevity tracking (década+)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Webinar model: $100K spend → $500-700K resultado
Co-living client: $70K spend → $400K+ primeiro mês → provável $600K
Call funnel test: $1,200 spend, 5 turds, 0 qualified
Pierre & Brez: $542K/mês aos 20 anos

Edward tax service: $50K → $400K economia
